title: 1.1 Introduction to Linux & the Terminal
reading:
Welcome to Linux!

Linux is a family of open-source Unix-like operating systems based on the Linux kernel. It is the backbone of the modern internet, powering servers, supercomputers, and smartphones.

The Command Line Interface (CLI)

While many Linux distributions offer a Graphical User Interface (GUI), the true power of Linux lies in the Command Line Interface (CLI) or Terminal.

  • Speed: CLI commands execute tasks faster than clicking through menus.
  • Automation: You can script commands to run automatically.
  • Remote Access: It is much easier and lighter to manage a remote server via SSH using the terminal.
💡 Note: When you open a terminal, you are presented with a shell prompt (often ending in a $ or #). This is where you type your commands.

quiz.q: What is the main advantage of using the Command Line Interface over a GUI?
opts: It has better graphics and animations
It provides faster execution and powerful automation capabilities
It requires a mouse to navigate

title: 1.2 Navigating the File System
reading:
The Linux Directory Structure

Unlike Windows, which uses drive letters like C:, Linux organizes all files in a single unified hierarchy starting from the root directory, denoted by a forward slash (/).

Basic Navigation Commands

  • pwd: Print Working Directory. Shows where you currently are.
  • ls: List directory contents.
  • cd: Change Directory. Move around the file system.
$ pwd
/home/user
$ ls
Desktop Documents Downloads
$ cd Documents
💡 Note: Use 'cd ..' to move up one directory, and 'cd ~' to return to your home directory.

title: 2.1 Managing Files and Directories
reading:
Creating Files and Directories

You can easily create new files and folders straight from the terminal.

  • mkdir: Make Directory. e.g., mkdir my_folder
  • touch: Create an empty file. e.g., touch myfile.txt

Copying, Moving, and Removing

  • cp: Copy files or directories. (Use -r for directories)
  • mv: Move or rename files. e.g., mv old.txt new.txt
  • rm: Remove files. e.g., rm file.txt
$ mkdir project
$ touch project/notes.txt
$ cp project/notes.txt project/backup.txt
$ rm project/notes.txt
💡 Note: Be extremely careful with the 'rm' command, especially 'rm -rf'. Once deleted, files usually cannot be recovered from the terminal!

quiz.q: Which command creates an empty file?

title: 2.2 Viewing File Contents
reading:
Reading Files from the Terminal

Linux provides several tools to read the contents of text files without opening a full-fledged text editor.

  • cat: Concatenate and print. Shows the entire file content at once. Great for short files.
  • less: Allows pagination. You can scroll up and down using arrow keys. Press 'q' to quit.
  • head: Outputs the first 10 lines of a file.
  • tail: Outputs the last 10 lines of a file. (Use 'tail -f' to watch a file update in real-time, great for logs!)
$ cat short_note.txt
Hello World!
$ tail -n 5 /var/log/syslog
💡 Note: If a file is very large, always use 'less' or 'tail' instead of 'cat' to prevent flooding your terminal screen.

quiz.q: Which command allows you to view a file page by page and scroll up or down?

title: 3.1 Understanding File Permissions
reading:
Linux File Permissions

Linux is a multi-user OS. Permissions define who can read, write, or execute a file.

Permissions are broken down into three categories:

  • User (u): The owner of the file.
  • Group (g): Users who belong to the file's group.
  • Others (o): Everyone else.

Changing Permissions with chmod

The chmod command changes file permissions. Permissions can be represented by letters (r=read, w=write, x=execute) or numbers (4=read, 2=write, 1=execute).

$ chmod +x script.sh # Adds execute permission
$ chmod 755 script.sh # Owner gets rwx (4+2+1=7), Group/Others get rx (4+1=5)
💡 Note: Use 'ls -l' to view the current permissions of files in a directory.

quiz.q: In the numeric permission '755', what does the '7' represent?
opts: Read and Write for the Owner
Read, Write, and Execute for the Owner
Read and Execute for the Group

title: 3.2 Searching for Files and Text
reading:
Finding Files

The find command is a powerful tool to search for files in a directory hierarchy.

$ find /home/user -name "*.txt"

Searching Text with grep

The grep command searches for patterns within files. It is arguably one of the most useful commands in Linux.

  • grep "error" log.txt: Finds lines containing "error".
  • grep -i "error" log.txt: Case-insensitive search.
  • grep -r "pattern" /dir: Recursively search inside a directory.
💡 Note: You can combine 'find' and 'grep' to search for specific text across multiple specific files!

quiz.q: Which command is used to search for a specific text pattern inside a file?

title: 4.1 Input, Output, and Piping
reading:
Standard Input and Output

In Linux, programs typically read from Standard Input (stdin) and write to Standard Output (stdout).

Redirection

You can redirect output to a file using > (overwrite) or >> (append).

$ echo "Hello" > greeting.txt
$ echo "World" >> greeting.txt

Piping

The pipe operator | takes the output of one command and passes it as input to another. This allows chaining commands together.

$ ls -l /var/log | grep "syslog"
$ cat names.txt | sort | uniq
💡 Note: Piping is a core philosophy of Unix/Linux: writing small programs that do one thing well and connecting them together.

quiz.q: What does the pipe (|) operator do?
opts: Passes the output of one command as input to another
Runs a command in the background

title: 4.2 Process Management
reading:
Monitoring Processes

Every running program in Linux is called a process. You can view running processes to monitor system health.

  • ps: Snapshot of current processes. (e.g., ps aux)
  • top: Interactive, real-time process viewer. (Press 'q' to quit)
  • htop: A more colorful and user-friendly version of top (often needs to be installed).

Managing Processes

If a process freezes, you can terminate it using the kill command followed by its Process ID (PID).

$ kill 1234
$ kill -9 1234 # Force kill
💡 Note: You can run a process in the background by appending an ampersand (&) to the end of your command. e.g., 'sleep 100 &'

quiz.q: Which command provides an interactive, real-time view of running processes?

title: 5.1 Archiving and Compressing
reading:
Working with Tarballs

In Linux, archiving (combining multiple files into one) and compressing (reducing file size) are often done together using the tar command.

  • Create an archive: tar -cvf archive.tar /folder
  • Extract an archive: tar -xvf archive.tar

Compression

To compress archives, we add flags for gzip (z) or bzip2 (j).

$ tar -czvf backup.tar.gz /home/user/Documents
$ tar -xzvf backup.tar.gz
💡 Note: The flags stand for: c (create), x (extract), z (gzip), v (verbose - see files processed), f (file).

quiz.q: Which flag is used with the 'tar' command to compress the archive using gzip?

title: 5.2 Package Management
reading:
Installing Software

Unlike Windows where you download .exe files from websites, Linux relies on Package Managers to download, install, and update software from official repositories.

Debian/Ubuntu (APT)

The Advanced Package Tool (APT) is standard on Debian-based systems.

  • Update package list: sudo apt update
  • Install a package: sudo apt install curl
  • Remove a package: sudo apt remove curl

Red Hat/CentOS (YUM/DNF)

These systems use yum or dnf.

$ sudo dnf install git
💡 Note: You usually need 'sudo' (superuser do) to install or remove software, as these commands affect the entire system.

quiz.q: Which command updates the list of available packages on an Ubuntu/Debian system?

title: 6.1 Networking Basics
reading:
Network Diagnostics

The terminal is perfect for troubleshooting network issues.

  • ping: Tests connectivity to another server. ping google.com
  • ifconfig / ip addr: Displays your network interfaces and IP addresses.

Downloading Files

You can fetch data or download files directly from the web.

  • curl: Fetch web content to the terminal. curl http://example.com
  • wget: Download files. wget http://example.com/file.zip
$ ping -c 4 8.8.8.8
$ wget https://kernel.org/pub/linux/kernel/v5.x/linux-5.10.tar.xz
💡 Note: Press Ctrl+C to stop commands like 'ping' that run continuously by default.

quiz.q: Which command is commonly used to download a file directly from the internet via the terminal?

title: 6.2 Text Editors in the Terminal
reading:
Why Terminal Editors?

When working on servers without a GUI, you must edit configuration files directly in the terminal.

Nano

nano is a simple, user-friendly editor. It displays shortcuts at the bottom of the screen.

$ nano config.txt
# Edit text, then press Ctrl+O to save, and Ctrl+X to exit.

Vim

vim is incredibly powerful but has a steep learning curve. It uses 'modes' (Command mode and Insert mode).

  • Open file: vim file.txt
  • Enter Insert Mode: Press i
  • Exit Insert Mode: Press Esc
  • Save and Quit: Type :wq and hit Enter.
💡 Note: If you ever get stuck in Vim, press 'Esc' multiple times, type ':q!', and press Enter to force quit without saving!

quiz.q: In Vim, how do you save your changes and quit the editor?

title: 7.1 Basic Bash Scripting
reading:
What is a Bash Script?

A Bash script is simply a plain text file containing a series of terminal commands. It allows you to automate repetitive tasks.

Creating a Script

Scripts typically start with a "shebang" (#!/bin/bash) to tell the system which interpreter to use.

#!/bin/bash
echo "Starting backup..."
tar -czvf backup.tar.gz /home/user/Documents
echo "Backup complete!"

Running the Script

Before running it, you must make it executable using chmod.

$ chmod +x backup.sh
$ ./backup.sh
💡 Note: The './' tells Linux to look for the script in the current directory, rather than in system paths.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of the 'shebang' (#!/bin/bash) at the top of a script?
opts: It makes the script run faster
It acts as a comment for documentation
It specifies the interpreter that should execute the script
